items.title: Minimal-API open-generic binding needs explicit [FromServices]/[FromBody]
items.description: When generating ASP.NET Core minimal-API endpoints over generic handlers, the model binder cannot always infer whether a parameter is a service or request body. Annotate service dependencies with [FromServices] and the payload with [FromBody] so the endpoint compiles and binds deterministically.
items.applicability: Code generation that emits minimal-API endpoint delegates for generic CRUD or command handlers.
items.example: app.MapPost('/api/{entity}', ([FromServices] IHandler h, [FromBody] CreateRequest r) => h.Handle(r)); without the attributes binding is ambiguous and fails at startup.
items.limitation: Explicit attributes are required mainly for ambiguous shapes; over-annotating simple primitive route values can be unnecessary but is harmless. Behaviour differs slightly across ASP.NET Core versions.

items.title: MVC controller, view, and route names must agree
items.description: Generated MVC artefacts fail at runtime when the controller name, the action, the view file path, and the route registration disagree. The generator must derive all four from a single canonical entity name so a ProductsController/Index action resolves Views/Products/Index.cshtml and the route maps correctly.
items.applicability: Generation of MVC controllers, Razor views, and route registrations in a generated ERP.
items.example: Entity 'Product' -> ProductsController, action Index, view Views/Products/Index.cshtml, route /Products. A mismatch (e.g. view under Views/Product/) yields a runtime view-not-found.
items.limitation: Pluralisation rules (Person/People, Category/Categories) must be applied consistently across all four artefacts; an inconsistent pluraliser is a common source of mismatch.

items.title: Template markers must sit on their own line
items.description: Code-generation template markers (insertion points, region begin/end) must be placed on their own line, not inline within emitted code. Inline markers cause the splice to break surrounding syntax (e.g. a marker mid-statement leaves a dangling token after replacement).
items.applicability: Template-based code generation that splices generated blocks into scaffold files.
items.example: Correct: a line '// <generated:properties>' alone; the generator replaces the whole line. Incorrect: 'public class X { // <generated:properties> }' which corrupts the class body on replace.
items.limitation: Marker-on-own-line conventions must be enforced by the template linter; if templates are hand-edited later the convention can silently regress.

items.title: EnsureCreated versus migrations for generated schemas
items.description: A generated ERP must choose one schema-provisioning strategy. EnsureCreated builds the schema once with no version history and cannot evolve it; migrations support incremental, versioned change. Mixing them corrupts the migrations history table. For an evolvable ERP, generate EF migrations and apply them on startup.
items.applicability: Schema provisioning code in a generated ERP backed by EF Core.
items.example: Use context.Database.Migrate() with generated migrations for production; EnsureCreated() is acceptable only for throwaway demos that never need a schema change.
items.limitation: Switching from EnsureCreated to migrations on an existing database requires baselining the schema; a naive switch fails because no migrations history exists.

items.title: Decimal money precision must be declared in the model
items.description: Generated entity money and quantity properties must declare an explicit decimal precision/scale (e.g. via HasPrecision(18,4) or a column type) so the database column is not created with a provider default that silently truncates or warns. Apply the same precision consistently across related columns.
items.applicability: Entity and EF configuration generation for any monetary or quantity field.
items.example: modelBuilder.Entity<Invoice>().Property(x => x.Amount).HasPrecision(18, 4); without it, EF/SQL Server may warn and default to a scale that loses cents.
items.limitation: Precision must match between operands of an aggregation; mismatched scales across joined columns can still lose precision even when each column is declared.

items.title: Cross-provider decimal aggregation must materialize first
items.description: Server-side SUM/AVG over decimal columns can lose precision or behave differently across providers (SQL Server vs SQLite vs in-memory used in tests). For correctness-critical totals, materialize the rows to the client and aggregate with .NET decimal, or assert the provider's behaviour explicitly in tests.
items.applicability: Generated reporting and total-computation queries that must match across the runtime and test providers.
items.example: Instead of relying on db.Lines.Sum(x => x.Amount) producing identical results everywhere, ToList() the line amounts then Sum() in memory for the document total used in balance checks.
items.limitation: Materializing large result sets to aggregate in memory does not scale; reserve this for bounded, correctness-critical totals, not for warehouse-scale reporting.

items.title: Local LLM proposes entities that collide with core
items.description: Local models generating a module spec frequently invent entities, tables, or namespaces whose names collide with existing core/framework types (e.g. User, Account, Item, Company). The generator must reconcile proposed names against a reserved-name list and the existing schema before emitting code.
items.applicability: Module-spec generation driven by a local LLM.
items.example: The model proposes an 'Account' entity that clashes with the platform's accounting Account; the collision guard renames it (e.g. CrmAccount) or maps it to the existing type.
items.limitation: Automatic renaming can confuse downstream references if not propagated everywhere; the reconciliation must rewrite all references, not just the declaration.

items.title: Local LLM wraps JSON output in markdown fences
items.description: Local models often wrap structured JSON responses in triple-backtick fences or add prose before/after the JSON. The spec parser must strip fences and surrounding text, then parse only the JSON object, and reject (with a retry) if no valid JSON is found.
items.applicability: Parsing of LLM-produced module specs and structured plans.
items.example: Response is ```json\n{...}\n```; the parser extracts the brace-delimited body, ignoring the fence and any commentary, before deserialising.
items.limitation: Naive fence-stripping breaks when the JSON legitimately contains backtick characters in string values; a brace-matching extractor is more robust than a regex on fences.

items.title: Reserved-name and existing-schema collision guard
items.description: Before emitting any generated entity, table, column, route, or namespace, validate the name against a reserved-keyword list, framework/core type names, and the current database schema. On collision, deterministically remap (prefix by module) and record the mapping so it is reproducible and reversible.
items.applicability: Name-resolution stage of the ERP generation pipeline.
items.example: Proposed table 'Order' collides with a SQL reserved word; the guard maps it to 'SalesOrder' and stores Order->SalesOrder in the generation manifest.
items.limitation: Reserved lists differ by database provider and language; the guard must be parameterised per target and kept current as the core schema grows.

items.title: Module spec defines entities, fields, types, and relationships explicitly
items.description: A generated module's spec must enumerate each entity with its fields, each field's data type and constraints (required, length, precision), and the relationships between entities (one-to-many, many-to-many, foreign keys) before any code is emitted. Code generation reads only from this validated spec.
items.applicability: The intermediate-representation stage between LLM proposal and code emission.
items.example: Spec: SalesOrder (1)->(many) SalesOrderLine; SalesOrderLine.Amount decimal(18,4) required; SalesOrder.CustomerId FK -> Customer.Id. Codegen consumes this, not free-form text.
items.limitation: A spec that omits relationship cardinality or nullability forces the generator to guess, which reintroduces inconsistency; the spec schema must make these fields mandatory.

items.title: Validate the spec before generating code
items.description: Run the module spec through a validation pass that checks: every entity has a primary key; every foreign key references an existing entity; field types are supported; required+default combinations are coherent; and no name collides with core or reserved words. Reject and re-prompt on failure rather than emitting code from a broken spec.
items.applicability: Gate between spec acceptance and code generation.
items.example: Validation rejects a spec where SalesOrderLine references OrderId but no Order/SalesOrder entity exists, triggering a targeted re-prompt to fix the reference.
items.limitation: Spec validation catches structural errors, not semantic ones (a valid-but-wrong business rule still passes); human or test-based review is still needed for correctness.

items.title: Generate list/detail/form UI from the entity spec
items.description: For each entity the generator emits a consistent UI triad: a list view (lightweight columns only), a detail/read view, and a create/edit form derived from the field spec, including validation attributes and data-testid hooks. List views must project only the columns needed, never full entities with large text fields.
items.applicability: UI generation for CRUD entities in a generated ERP.
items.example: Customer spec yields a list (Name, Code, Status), a detail page, and a form with required-name validation and data-testid='customer-save'; the list query selects only the three columns.
items.limitation: Auto-generated forms handle scalar and simple FK fields well but need manual extension for complex composite or grid (line-item) editing such as invoice lines.

items.title: Generate REST API with consistent contract per entity
items.description: The generator emits a consistent REST surface per entity (list with paging, get-by-id, create, update, submit/cancel for documents) with uniform status codes, problem-details errors, and authorization attributes wired from the RBAC spec. Document endpoints expose lifecycle transitions rather than raw status edits.
items.applicability: API generation for entities and documents in a generated ERP.
items.example: GET /api/customers?page=1, GET /api/customers/{id}, POST /api/customers, PUT /api/customers/{id}; POST /api/invoices/{id}/submit transitions the lifecycle and posts.
items.limitation: A uniform CRUD contract under-serves complex operations (bulk actions, reports); these need bespoke endpoints rather than forcing them through the generated CRUD shape.

items.title: Score generated module against a reference feature checklist
items.description: Parity scoring compares a generated module against a checklist of expected capabilities (entities present, CRUD works, postings balanced, approvals enforced, reports render) and produces a percentage plus a list of missing items. The score drives the autonomous fix loop and gates release.
items.applicability: Quality measurement of a generated ERP module.
items.example: Module scores 85%: missing items = 'approval matrix not wired', 'no audit on cancel'. The fix loop targets exactly those gaps, then re-scores.
items.limitation: Parity scores reflect only what the checklist measures; a high score against a shallow checklist gives false confidence. Checklists must include invariant assertions, not just presence checks.

items.title: Weight parity by invariant criticality
items.description: Not all checklist items are equal: a balanced-ledger or negative-stock-guard invariant must weigh more than a cosmetic field. Parity scoring should weight critical invariants heavily so a module cannot reach a passing score while a core financial control is broken.
items.applicability: Design of the parity-scoring rubric for generated ERPs.
items.example: Ledger-balance and RBAC checks carry blocking weight: failing either caps the score below the release threshold regardless of how many cosmetic items pass.
items.limitation: Weighting is a judgement call that must be reviewed; mis-weighting can either block on trivia or pass on a real defect, so the rubric needs periodic calibration against real failures.

items.title: Validate a generated ERP with build + test + Playwright + run gate
items.description: A generated ERP is only accepted when it passes a four-stage gate: (1) it compiles in Release, (2) unit tests including invariant tests pass, (3) Playwright E2E smoke and scenario tests pass with no HTTP 500, and (4) the app actually starts and core pages return quickly. A green build alone is necessary but not sufficient.
items.applicability: Acceptance gate for any module or full ERP produced by the generator.
items.example: Pipeline: dotnet build -c Release; dotnet test; npx playwright test; start app and curl core routes asserting <1s and status<400. Any stage failing blocks acceptance.
items.limitation: The gate proves the build runs and invariants hold for tested paths; it does not prove the full business design is correct, so domain review remains necessary.

items.title: Run startup and page-load probe to catch runtime hangs
items.description: Because the most damaging defects are runtime hangs (not build errors), the validation gate must start the generated app and probe each core page for a fast response. A page that logs 'started' with no matching 'completed' indicates a stall and must fail the gate, mirroring LocalAIFactory's own runtime-audit discipline.
items.applicability: Runtime validation stage of the generation gate.
items.example: Probe each route with a timeout; if any route exceeds the budget or never completes, fail acceptance and surface the stalled path from the request-timing log.
items.limitation: A passing probe on seeded data does not guarantee performance at scale; the probe validates absence of hangs on representative data, not production load behaviour.

items.title: Avoid GroupBy-constant aggregation in generated queries
items.description: Generated list and dashboard queries must not use GroupBy(_ => 1) or other group-by-constant aggregate projections, which have produced indefinite page hangs on SQL Server. Emit separate CountAsync calls and project list rows to lightweight records selecting only needed columns.
items.applicability: Query generation for list views, dashboards, and summary cards.
items.example: Instead of one GroupBy-constant query returning multiple counts, generate distinct CountAsync() calls and a projection to a record DTO without large text columns.
items.limitation: Multiple round-trips are slightly chattier than a single aggregate; this trade is deliberate because the single clever aggregate has repeatedly hung in this estate.

items.title: Never materialize large text columns in generated list views
items.description: Generated list queries must project to a lightweight record that selects only the columns the list shows, never the full entity, because large text columns (descriptions, raw content, message bodies) cause unbounded client-side materialization that the SQL command timeout does not catch.
items.applicability: List-query generation for any entity carrying large text fields.
items.example: For a Notes entity, the list selects (Id, Title, CreatedUtc) into a record; the large Body column is loaded only on the detail page, not in the list.
items.limitation: Requires the generator to know which columns are large; without column-size metadata in the spec it cannot reliably exclude them, so the spec must flag large fields.

items.title: Local LLM invents non-existent framework APIs
items.description: Local models sometimes call methods or attributes that do not exist in the target framework version (plausible-looking but invented). The build stage of the gate catches these, but the generator should also constrain emission to a known API surface and re-prompt with the compiler error on failure.
items.applicability: Code emission and the build-fix loop in the generator.
items.example: The model calls a non-existent DbContext.BulkSave(); the build fails, and the fix loop feeds the exact compiler error back to the model to correct the call.
items.limitation: Feeding raw compiler errors back works for simple cases but can loop on systematic misconceptions; a capped retry count plus a known-API constraint is needed to avoid infinite loops.

items.title: Route and naming-series collisions across modules
items.description: When multiple generated modules coexist, route prefixes and document naming-series prefixes can collide (two modules both claiming /orders or the SO- prefix). The generator must allocate unique route and series prefixes per module and verify uniqueness across the whole generated application.
items.applicability: Multi-module generation where modules are produced independently then combined.
items.example: Sales and Service both propose /orders; the guard namespaces them to /sales/orders and /service/orders and assigns distinct series prefixes SO- and SVO-.
items.limitation: Namespacing routes changes URLs that any hand-written client or test may have hardcoded; the mapping must be published so consumers can adjust.

items.title: Autonomous fix loop must be bounded and evidence-driven
items.description: The autonomous fix loop that repairs a generated ERP must run with a capped iteration budget, act only on concrete evidence (compiler errors, failing test names, parity gaps), and stop when the gate passes or the budget is exhausted, surfacing the remaining failures rather than looping silently.
items.applicability: Orchestration of the generate-validate-fix cycle.
items.example: Loop up to N iterations: build/test/score, feed the specific failures back, regenerate the affected artefacts, re-validate; on budget exhaustion, report the unresolved gate failures for human review.
items.limitation: An unbounded or evidence-free loop wastes local compute and can oscillate between two broken states; the budget and the requirement for concrete evidence are what make it terminate.
